title: Strengthen the Brethren
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Strengthen the Brethren - Pastor Tito Lyro - Hebrews 12_12-17.txt

outline.point: I. The Christian Life Is Movement Toward Christ Through Suffering
subpoints: A. Progressive sanctification: the shorter catechism defines it as renewal in the whole man, dying to sin and living to righteousness
B. The book of Hebrews is addressed to a congregation under persecution, considering leaving the faith
C. Suffering is designed to help us be conformed to Christ — not joyful now, but yielding the peaceful fruit of righteousness (v. 11)
D. The metaphor: we are running a race with a cloud of witnesses cheering us on (12:1)

outline.point: II. Strengthen — Raise Up the Hands That Hang Down (v. 12)
subpoints: A. The word 'strengthen' means to make straight again — the verb form behind 'ortho' in orthodontics, orthopedics
B. Drooping hands and feeble knees: vivid picture of spiritual paralysis from the weight of suffering
C. The solution is corporate — the plural shows that all runners are to come alongside the collapsed runner
D. Isaiah 35:3-4: how do we strengthen the weak? By pointing them to the cross and the coming again of Christ

outline.point: III. Make Straight — Level the Path for the Lame (v. 13)
subpoints: A. Going ahead of the handicapped runner to clear obstacles from the path — like John the Baptist preparing the way
B. This was only ever done for kings in the Old Testament — the lame is treated as royalty in Christ
C. How do we make the path straight? By proclaiming the gospel daily — the message of repentance and grace in Christ
D. As John the Baptist said of Jesus: 'Behold the Lamb of God who takes away the sins of the world'

outline.point: IV. Pursue — Chase After Peace and Holiness (v. 14)
subpoints: A. A categorical command to pursue peace with all people — including enemies
B. Peace is not the absence of conflict but the presence of goodwill, forgiveness, and other-centeredness
C. The pursuit of peace must never compromise holiness — without holiness no one will see the Lord
D. Both purity and peace must go together; every church officer swears to maintain both

outline.point: V. The Danger of Neglect: Bitterness, Immorality, and Esau's Loss (vv. 15-17)
subpoints: A. When we fail to strengthen, make straight, and pursue peace — bitterness takes root and defiles many
B. Esau's example: forfeiting the eternal birthright for a momentary comfort — and finding no place for repentance
C. We are responsible for each other's race — the goal is that we all arrive at the finish line together
D. God's appointed means for perseverance include the encouragement of fellow believers

summary:
Preaching from Hebrews 12:12-17, Pastor Lyro presents the Christian life as dynamic, constant movement toward holiness — what the catechism calls progressive sanctification. The letter to the Hebrews was written to a congregation being persecuted and considering abandoning the faith. The Holy Spirit's response is not to minimize suffering but to show it is God-designed chastening that yields the peaceful fruit of righteousness. The three-fold command of the passage — strengthen the drooping hands (v. 12), make straight the paths (v. 13), pursue peace and holiness (v. 14) — form the sermon's structure, each drawn from Old Testament allusions the Hebrew-Christian audience would recognize.

The sermon's most distinctive emphasis is corporate responsibility. The Greek words throughout these verses are plural: all the runners share responsibility for keeping their fellow runners going. Drawing on the imagery of a marathon runner who collapses before the finish line, and on John Owen's illustration of sticks bound together by a rope, Pastor Lyro argues that the church functions as a team where no one is left behind. The means of strengthening are specific: pointing fellow believers to the cross of Christ, to his intercession, and to his coming again. The sermon closes with a warning from verses 15-17: when the church fails to come alongside its members in suffering, bitterness takes root, defiles many, and can lead to forfeiting grace — Esau's tragic example of trading eternal birthright for momentary comfort.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Hebrews 12:12-17

If you're able to remain standing for a scripture reading this morning, turn to the book of Hebrews chapter 12. We're going to read verses 12 through 17 as we consider the Christian life as a life of movement towards holiness.

So this is the word of our Lord, Hebrews chapter 12, starting at verse 12.

Therefore strengthen the hands which hang down, and the feeble knees, and make straight paths for your feet, so that what is lame may not be dislocated, but rather be healed. Pursue peace with all people, and holiness, without which no one will see the Lord, looking carefully lest anyone fall short of the grace of God; lest any root of bitterness springing up cause trouble, and by this many become defiled; lest there be any fornicator or profane person like Esau, who for one morsel of food sold his birthright. For you know that afterward, when he wanted to inherit the blessing, he was rejected, for he found no place for repentance, though he sought it diligently with tears.

This is the word of our Lord. Let us pray together. Father in heaven, thank you for your word. We pray that your Spirit would take hold of your word and take hold of our hearts, and open our eyes to see wonderful things concerning you in your word. We pray in Jesus' name, amen. Please be seated.

## Introduction: The Christian Life Is Movement

The life of the Christian is a dynamic life. Everything in his life, everything in her life, is moving toward Christ. We are all about change — change is the norm in the Christian life. And that can be difficult for a lot of us who like things to stay constant, who like things to stay the same.

This idea of the Christian life as constant movement and change is what we call progressive sanctification. Our Shorter Catechism defines the process this way: "Sanctification is a work of God's free grace whereby we are renewed in the whole man after the image of God, and are enabled more and more to die unto sin and live unto righteousness."

The life of the Christian is just that process. The grace of God is working in us so that everything that happens to us has the goal of causing us to be more holy, more like the Lord Jesus Christ — and that includes suffering in our lives.

The book of Hebrews is written to a congregation that is suffering persecution — and not for the first time. This congregation has suffered persecution several times, and now they're considering leaving the faith because things are really hard. They think: things were better before we came to Christ. Can't we just go back to Judaism? We're still going to worship God, we'll still go to the temple, but we can't bear anymore under the suffering that this faith has brought upon us.

So suffering in the life of this congregation was threatening its movement toward Christ. The Holy Spirit reminds them that even suffering comes from the hand of a good, loving, and merciful God. Look at verses 5-6: "My son, do not despise the chastening of the Lord, nor be discouraged when you are rebuked by Him; for whom the Lord loves He chastens, and scourges every son whom He receives."

The Holy Spirit tells us that suffering is designed to help us be conformed to Jesus Christ. Even though it is not pleasant — verse 11: "Now no chastening seems to be joyful for the present, but painful; nevertheless, afterward it yields the peaceable fruit of righteousness to those who have been trained by it." Suffering has a design. It is to make you more like Jesus Christ.

I think the Christian worldview gives far more meaning to suffering than the view that says there is no God. If everything happens at random, then whatever you're going through means absolutely nothing. Yet the scriptures tell us that whatever suffering we go through, we do so because Christ is shaping us to be more like him.

And because suffering is designed to propel us toward Christ, the Holy Spirit tells this church: get up and move. Don't sit there and wallow in your suffering. Get up and move. And this passage comes in the context of that race the Christian is running — verse 1 of chapter 12 tells us that we have this cloud of witnesses who have gone before us, cheering us on, saying: Christ has done it for you. We've arrived at the end. You can do it as well.

## II. Strengthen the Drooping Hands (v. 12)

So the Spirit exhorts us in verses 12-17 to keep on going, and this exhortation is built on three allusions to Old Testament passages. The book of Hebrews is written to a Jewish-Christian church and is filled with Old Testament references.

Verse 12 alludes to Isaiah 35:3-4: "Strengthen the weak hands, and make firm the feeble knees. Say to those who are fearful-hearted, 'Be strong, do not fear! Behold, your God will come with vengeance, with the recompense of God; He will come and save you.'" Verse 13 alludes to Proverbs 4:26: "Ponder the path of your feet, and let all your ways be established." And verse 14 would trigger Psalm 34:14: "Depart from evil and do good; seek peace and pursue it."

Look again at verse 12: "Therefore strengthen the hands which hang down."

The idea communicated by this word "strengthen" is of strengthening by making straight again — and it's the same root from which we get the English prefix *ortho*: orthodontics (making teeth straight), orthopedics (making bones straight), orthodoxy (having right doctrine before God). When Jesus healed someone who was physically crooked, Luke says "he laid his hands on her, and immediately she was made straight" — that's the same word as here.

Can you picture in your mind someone having drooping hands? The description of hands hanging down to the side of the body is a very vivid image of discouragement — not being ready to compete or to do anything. And the same goes for "feeble knees" — not just weak knees, but knees that won't function at all. The word is used elsewhere for "paralyzed" or "paralytic." Remember when friends brought the paralyzed man to Jesus through the roof of the house — the word for that paralytic is the same as the "feeble knees" here.

So picture this: someone is running a race, and somewhere in the middle they collapse and go limp. I think it was the 1988 Olympics where a woman in the women's marathon was the last to arrive in the stadium. She came in, and she just couldn't do it anymore — before the finish line, she went down. That's the idea. That's how the Spirit sees the Hebrew church, and that's how the Spirit sees the Christian who, because of the pressures of life, has come to a point of not being able to run the race anymore.

Leon Morris said: "The exhortation implies that the readers are acting as those spiritually paralyzed. They are urged to put things right and get moving."

### How Do We Strengthen the Weak?

And notice — the word "strengthen" is a plural. This means *all of us* are to strengthen the hands that are drooping. We all go alongside the runner who has collapsed. You ever seen videos of people doing that in races — a runner falls down and another stops to help him up, and they finish the race together? That's the idea here.

This corporate responsibility for each other's faith and perseverance is stressed throughout the book of Hebrews. Look at chapter 3:13: "Exhort one another daily, while it is called 'Today,' lest any of you be hardened through the deceitfulness of sin." Look at chapter 10:24-25: "Consider one another in order to stir up love and good works, not forsaking the assembling of ourselves together, as is the manner of some, but exhorting one another, and so much the more as you see the Day approaching."

The model of the Marines is "Leave no man behind." That's really the model of Christianity as well. We only win if we all arrive together at the finish line.

Now, how do we strengthen the weak? The Old Testament passage that verse 12 alludes to — Isaiah 35:3-4 — tells us. Isaiah says: "Strengthen the weak hands, make firm the feeble knees. Say to those who are fearful-hearted: Be strong! Do not fear! Behold, your God will come with vengeance — He will come and save you." How do you do it? By giving them this message: behold your God — point them to Christ.

Specifically, we point them to the cross. How the cross has vindicated us. How God has already taken vengeance on our sins at the cross. How the Father has taken vengeance on our sins in Christ. How we have been saved from sin, from Satan, and from the wrath of God. How we can persevere through suffering because Christ persevered through the cross and rose again on the third day.

But we also point them to the coming again of Christ. This life is not all that there is. This life at times will stink, and there is real suffering in it — and if this were all there was, as Jean-Paul Sartre said, the logical conclusion would be suicide. But this is not all there is. Christ is coming back. We have an eternal home. We are going to be resurrected and forever with our Lord. That's what strengthens the weak.

## III. Make Straight the Path for the Lame (v. 13)

The same idea is now communicated with a different word picture. Instead of stronger runners coming alongside the one who has collapsed, they are going ahead of the handicapped runner, making sure the track is straight — not without curves, but without bumps.

Picture the Paralympics. You have a blind runner or a runner without legs, and these helpers go ahead of the handicapped runner making sure the path is smooth. They go before, planing the way, so that the runner won't stumble — like the Messiah passage from Handel: "every valley shall be lifted up, every hill be brought low."

And it's interesting: in the Old Testament, this idea of making the path straight was only done on behalf of kings. The servants of the king would go ahead and make his journey smooth. Isn't that what John the Baptist does for Jesus — he came to prepare the way, to make the way plain for the Lord Jesus?

Now the lame person isn't a king — but the lame is *important* to the strong, to the point that the strong go ahead of the weak and work hard at making the way plain until the lame is strong enough to deal with the issues of life more directly.

How do we make the path straight? Verses 15-17 tell us specifically, but we can also learn from John the Baptist. What did he do to prepare the way for King Jesus? He proclaimed the gospel. And we tend to think the gospel is only for getting saved — and then we go do something else. But it is the gospel of Jesus Christ that keeps us going through life. It is the gospel of daily repentance as we grow in the Lord.

What was John the Baptist's message as he stood in the Jordan River, baptizing, when he looked up and saw Jesus coming? "Behold the Lamb of God who takes away the sins of the world!" In moments of weakness, in every moment of life actually — but especially in moments of weakness and suffering — we need to be reminded: we have the Lord Jesus, who has taken away the guilt of our sins and brought us together with the Father, and now we have eternal fellowship with him. The enemy may be attacking hard, trials may be coming, whatever is going on in this world — Jesus Christ sticks closer than a brother. He's the Friend who gave his life for us, our elder Brother who has gone before us and enables us to keep going.

## IV. Pursue Peace and Holiness (v. 14)

The third command in this triad is verse 14: "Pursue peace with all people, and holiness, without which no one will see the Lord."

This is a categorical command to do whatever you can to be at peace with all people. "All" means all — including our enemies. This reflects the teaching of our Lord: "You have heard that it was said, 'You shall love your neighbor and hate your enemy.' But I say to you, love your enemies, pray for those who persecute you."

Peace is the absence of strife and contention, the absence of enmity and bitterness, the absence of anger. It is also the presence of goodwill toward the people around us, the ability to forgive and ask for forgiveness, and the lack of self-focus in favor of focus on others.

As Paul says in Romans 14:19 in the context of disagreements about meat: "Let us pursue the things which make for peace and the things by which one may edify one another." And how do we do that? If you think eating meat is fine, great — but for the sake of others, go a season without it. The focus should not be on yourself but on how you can serve someone else.

One of the things that may have been paralyzing the congregation in Hebrews was their desire for revenge — their animosity toward those who were persecuting them. The Holy Spirit says: to move forward in the race of life, you must pursue peace, not revenge.

This pursuit is a present imperative — it's a constant, ongoing pursuit. And this is a characteristic of the sons and daughters of God: "Blessed are the peacemakers, for they shall be called sons of God."

But notice: the pursuit of peace with all people is not to be done at the expense of holiness. Peace is not the ultimate goal in life — the glory of God is. Peace is a means to declare God's glory, and therefore it must be pursued according to God's terms. Peace is only godly peace when the love of God has not been violated in achieving it. If holiness is compromised, then the peace is not the peace of God that passes all understanding but the peace of Satan that leads to judgment. That's why verse 14 links them: pursue peace *and holiness* — without which no one will see the Lord.

## V. The Danger of Neglect (vv. 15-17)

When we fail to do these three things — strengthen, make straight, pursue peace — what happens? Verses 15-17 describe the danger.

"Looking carefully lest anyone fall short of the grace of God; lest any root of bitterness springing up cause trouble, and by this many become defiled; lest there be any fornicator or profane person like Esau, who for one morsel of food sold his birthright. For you know that afterward, when he wanted to inherit the blessing, he was rejected, for he found no place for repentance, though he sought it diligently with tears."

The words "looking carefully" are also in the plural — all of us are to exercise oversight over one another in the race of life, making sure we all get to the end. In one way, we are responsible for each other's salvation. We are supposed to make sure that we all arrive at the end.

Notice the three "lests" in these verses. When we do not pay attention to these three exhortations, we are in danger of missing the grace of God. When we are not in each other's lives making sure we are moving together toward the finish line, we are in danger of letting bitterness take root. That root becomes the governing principle of everything we do — a life directed by bitterness, resentment, and the desire for revenge. And that bitterness defiles many.

Esau's example is sobering: he forfeited the eternal birthright — his spiritual inheritance — for one momentary meal. And afterward, when he wanted to inherit the blessing, he found no place for repentance, though he sought it diligently with tears. The point is not that repentance was unavailable, but that the opportunity had passed. The moment of decision had come and gone.

Now, those who are truly in Jesus Christ will persevere to the end — that is certain. If you have been redeemed by the Lord Jesus Christ, you will arrive at the end; you will come into the arms of Jesus. But one of the means God has appointed for us to persevere is his people coming alongside us and helping us in our race.

Two sides of this truth: one, we must allow God's people into our lives. No man is an island. We must allow people in so they can help us pursue Christ. Two, we must pursue coming alongside others to help them pursue Christ. You must be interested in other people. If you're only interested in yourself, that is not evidence that you know Christ. Evidence of knowing Christ is that you are interested in other people, that your life is not just about yourself.

So we cannot afford being paralyzed in our spiritual life — we have to keep moving forward. And we can't afford allowing others to be paralyzed either. Either we all arrive at the end, or none of us do. We need to be in this together. We must strengthen each other. We must make the way clear for each other. We must pursue peace together so that we all cross the finish line and hear from our Savior: "Well done, my good and faithful servant — enter into the rest prepared for you before the foundation of the world."

Let's pray together. Father in heaven, thank you that you have saved us to be part of the church, and that together we're moving toward Christ. Enable us — give us grace and strength to be an encouragement to each other, and to be instruments in your hands to help each other arrive at the end. We ask these things in Jesus' name, amen.
